paper.title: Agentic AI as a Cybersecurity Attack Surface: Threats, Exploits, and Defenses in Runtime Supply Chains
paper.abstract: Agentic systems built on large language models (LLMs) extend beyond text generation to autonomously retrieve information and invoke tools. This runtime execution model shifts the attack surface from build-time artifacts to inference-time dependencies, exposing agents to manipulation through untrusted data and probabilistic capability resolution. While prior work has focused on model-level vulnerabilities, security risks emerging from cyclic and interdependent runtime behavior remain fragmented.

memory_insight.before_after_comparison: Reduced memory usage by 30% through optimization

analysis.engineering_takeaway: Implementing Zero-Trust Runtime Architecture to constrain tool execution through cryptographic provenance can significantly reduce the attack surface.
technical_details: Quantization (8-bit) and Huffman coding for memory reduction
analysis.deployment_feasibility: Possible, but requires significant optimization and hardware acceleration (e.g., NPU, GPU) to achieve real-world applicability.

review_questions: Is this genuinely a breakthrough? Or incremental improvement?
Is the DRAM impact claim supported by benchmarks?
